subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove or give a counterexample: \(\lVert A\rVert_2 \leq \sqrt{\lVert A\rVert_\infty \lVert A\rVert_1}\). If you prove this, make sure to justify each nontrivial step.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove or give a counterexample: \(\lVert A\rVert_2 \leq \lVert A\rVert_F\), where \(\lVert A\rVert_F\) is the Frobenius norm of \(A\). If you prove this, make sure to justify each nontrivial step.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that the inverse of an upper-triangular matrix is upper-triangular.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Let \(A \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times n}\) with \(m>n\). Consider the least-squares problem of finding \(x \in \mathbb{C}^n\) that minimizes \(\lVert Ax-b\rVert\) in the 2-norm. Describe a method for solving the problem efficiently, and explain (and justify) why the normal equations should not be solved.

problems.text: Let \(A \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times n}\), with \(m \geq n\) and \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=p=n \geq 3\). Let \(a_1,a_2,\ldots\) denote the columns of \(A\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Using the classical Gram–Schmidt process, write out expressions for \(q_1,q_2,q_3\), the first three columns of \(Q\) in the \(QR\) decomposition of \(A\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show the vector \(q_3\) found in part (a) is orthogonal to both \(q_1\) and \(q_2\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Write an expression for the first Householder reflector \(H_1\), used to find the \(QR\) decomposition of \(A\). Show \(H_1\) is both unitary and Hermitian.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Show that all eigenvalues of \(A\) are real.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text:
Define the stationary iterative method (a.k.a. fixed point method)
\[
x^{(k+1)}=Ax^{(k)}+b. \tag{1}
\]
Suppose (1) has fixed-point \(x\), namely \(x\) satisfies \(x=Ax+b\). Show the iteration (1) converges to \(x\) from any starting guess \(x^{(0)}\), that is \(x^{(k)} \to x\) as \(k \to \infty\), if and only if the eigenvalues \(\lambda_i\) of \(A\) satisfy \(|\lambda_i|<1\), \(i=1,\ldots,m\). You may use the fact that Hermitian matrix \(A\) is unitarily diagonalizable.

problems.text:
Consider the matrix \(A\) given by
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & -1 & 2 & 0 \\
-1 & 4 & -1 & 1 \\
2 & -1 & 6 & -2 \\
0 & 1 & -2 & 4
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Suppose the eigenvalues of \(A\) are all distinct (they are) and satisfy \(\lambda_1>\lambda_2>\lambda_3>\lambda_4\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Describe an algorithm that could be used to approximate \(\lambda_4\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Describe algorithms that could be used to approximate \(\lambda_2,\lambda_3\), and their eigenvectors.
